worth_it_explanation: Authorized Buyers, the demand formerly branded Ad Exchange, is usually the largest single programmatic demand source on a Google Ad Manager network, and it competes through dynamic allocation. Whether it is enabled and how tightly it is gated has an outsized effect on revenue, so a short audit of the demand it is allowed to bring carries real money. Both checks read from screens already in the account, the inventory and the brand-safety protections, and neither needs new data or a rebuild. Loosening a stale block or opting inventory back into competition can lift clearing prices from the day it ships.
intro: Authorized Buyers is the real-time demand that Google Ad Manager brings to a publisher's inventory, the demand formerly branded Ad Exchange (AdX). It competes through dynamic allocation, where its live bids are weighed against the publisher's own line items on every impression so the highest value wins. These checks confirm that Authorized Buyers is actually competing on the inventory that should monetize programmatically, and that the protective block lists guarding that inventory are no broader than the brand-safety need that put them there.

audit_checks.title: Authorized Buyers demand is not competing through dynamic allocation
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Authorized Buyers is typically the largest single programmatic demand source on a Google Ad Manager network. When it is not enabled, or the inventory is not opted into competing, the publisher forgoes that demand on every affected impression, which lowers fill and clearing price on inventory that should monetize programmatically.

educate.base:
Authorized Buyers is the real-time demand that Google Ad Manager (GAM) brings to a publisher's inventory, the demand formerly branded Ad Exchange (AdX). It reaches the inventory through dynamic allocation, the mechanism that lets non-guaranteed demand bid in real time against the publisher's own line items so the impression goes to whichever source pays the most. A bid from Authorized Buyers serves only when it beats the temporary value GAM calculates for a competing line item, which protects guaranteed delivery while still capturing the higher price when the live bid is higher.

Because Authorized Buyers is usually the largest single programmatic demand source on a GAM network, inventory that is not opted into this competition leaves that demand out of the auction entirely. The impression still fills from a line item or house ad, but it fills at the booked rate rather than at the real-time price a buyer was willing to pay. The gap does not appear as an error. It appears as lower programmatic revenue on inventory that looks like it is delivering.

Two conditions have to hold for the demand to compete. Ad Exchange has to be active on the network, and the ad units or line items serving the inventory have to allow real-time bids to compete rather than being locked to direct demand. A network can have Authorized Buyers active while a slice of its inventory, a new ad unit or a section reorganized in the UI, never got opted into competition.

A few signs point to Authorized Buyers not competing on inventory that should monetize programmatically:
  • An ad unit fills almost entirely from direct line items or house ads with little or no Open Auction delivery
  • Programmatic revenue is concentrated on older ad units while newer ones show none
  • The network has Ad Exchange active, yet a section of inventory reports no real-time bid competition
You may want to confirm both that Ad Exchange is active and that the specific inventory in question is set to let real-time demand compete. One without the other leaves the demand on the sidelines.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, programmatic demand is often the bulk of the revenue on run-of-site display, so an ad unit that is not opted into competition fills at a lower rate. You may want to check that newer ad units and any section reorganized in the UI carry the same competition settings as the units that already monetize well.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, programmatic display usually sits alongside higher-value direct and sponsored placements, so it is easy to leave a programmatic unit out of competition without noticing. You may want to confirm the display inventory that is meant to monetize through the Open Auction is actually letting Authorized Buyers compete rather than holding for direct demand that may not fill it.

learn_more.source_title: Use dynamic allocation to optimize every impression

investigate.intro: Confirm that Ad Exchange is active on the network and that the ad units and line items serving the inventory that should monetize programmatically are set to let Authorized Buyers compete in the Open Auction.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Admin', then 'Global settings', and confirm Ad Exchange is active on the network. If Ad Exchange is not present at all, Authorized Buyers demand is not available to compete.

Open 'Inventory', then 'Ad units', and pick an ad unit that should monetize programmatically. Note whether it is set to allow Open Auction and Authorized Buyers demand rather than being restricted to direct line items only.

Repeat for newer ad units and any section recently reorganized in the UI, comparing their settings to a unit that already earns programmatic revenue. Record any unit where real-time demand is not allowed to compete.

gam_reporting.label: Google Ad Manager reporting
steps: Open 'Reporting' and build a report broken out by ad unit, with an Ad Exchange or Open Auction revenue metric alongside total impressions.
Identify ad units that fill but show little or no Open Auction or Authorized Buyers revenue. Record each one as a candidate where the demand may not be competing.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad unit that should monetize programmatically, whether Ad Exchange is active on the network and whether the unit is set to let Authorized Buyers compete in the Open Auction, supported by the reporting check for real-time revenue. An ad unit on an Ad Exchange-active network that allows real-time demand to compete and shows Open Auction delivery passes. An ad unit that is locked to direct demand, or that shows no real-time competition on a network where Ad Exchange is active, is a fail recorded with the ad unit name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether Ad Exchange is active and whether each programmatic ad unit allows Authorized Buyers to compete above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that Authorized Buyers demand is not competing through dynamic allocation on part of {{site_name}}'s inventory. Authorized Buyers, the demand formerly branded Ad Exchange (AdX), is usually the largest single programmatic demand source on a Google Ad Manager network, and inventory that is not opted into competing fills at the booked rate rather than at the real-time price a buyer would pay, so programmatic revenue runs lower than the inventory could earn. {{voice_recommend}} confirming Ad Exchange is active on the network and opting the affected ad units and line items into the Open Auction so real-time bids compete against direct demand. After the change, watch the Open Auction revenue on those ad units in reporting to confirm the demand is now competing.

audit_checks.title: Buyer or brand block lists are broader than intended
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Protective blocks are necessary, but a block list that grew broader than its original brand-safety reason shrinks the pool of buyers competing for each impression. Fewer competing bids depress clearing prices on the affected inventory, and the loss is invisible because the blocked demand simply never shows up in the auction.

educate.base:
Google Ad Manager (GAM) gives a publisher several protective controls over the demand it accepts, grouped under Protections. These include blocked buyers, blocked advertisers and brands, and sensitive or general category blocks. Each control exists for a reason, keeping a competitor's ads off the page, honoring an advertiser exclusion, or holding back categories that do not fit the audience. The controls work by removing the blocked demand from the auction before it can bid.

The tension is that every block also removes a potential bidder. Authorized Buyers competes by bringing many buyers to each impression, and the clearing price is set by the competition among them. A block list that has grown broader than the brand-safety reason that put it there, an advertiser blocked for a campaign that ended, a buyer blocked during an incident that is long resolved, or a category blocked network-wide when only one section needed it, thins that competition and lowers the price the remaining bidders have to beat.

This loss does not surface as an error. The blocked demand never appears in reporting, so there is nothing to flag. The only way to tell whether a block still earns its place is to read the list and ask, for each entry, whether the reason that created it still applies and whether the block is scoped to the inventory that actually needs it. Block lists in GAM apply to the Open Auction and First Look, the demand Authorized Buyers brings, so a broad block lands directly on the largest programmatic source.

A few patterns suggest a block list has drifted wider than intended:
  • Advertiser or brand blocks with no recorded reason or owner, so no one can say why they exist
  • Buyer blocks added during a past incident that has since been resolved
  • A category or advertiser blocked network-wide when only one section or audience needed the protection
You may want to review each active block for a current, documented reason and confirm it is scoped no wider than the inventory the brand-safety need actually covers.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, blocks often accumulate across years and editors, so a list can carry advertiser and category blocks whose original reason no one remembers. You may want to confirm each block still reflects a current editorial or brand-safety standard, and that a category held back for one sensitive section is not blocked across the whole network.
site_type_overlays.healthcare: On a health or medical site, category and advertiser blocks are frequently set wide to avoid sensitive or non-compliant ads near medical content. You may want to confirm the blocks are scoped to the inventory that genuinely needs them, so general inventory is not carrying restrictions meant only for the clinical sections.

investigate.intro: Open the brand-safety protections and review the buyer, advertiser, and brand blocks for entries that are stale or scoped wider than the brand-safety need that created them.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Brand safety', then 'Protections', and review the active ad content protections that block buyers, advertisers, or brands.
For each block, note its targeting, whether it applies network-wide or to specific inventory, and whether a reason or owner is recorded for it.

Open the advertiser and brand block lists and the buyer block lists, and record any entry with no documented reason, any tied to a campaign or incident that has ended, and any applied network-wide that only one section needed.
Check the general and sensitive category blocks the same way, noting categories blocked across the whole network where only part of the inventory required the protection.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active block, whether a current reason for it still applies and whether its scope is no broader than the inventory the brand-safety need covers. A block with a documented, still-valid reason that is scoped to the inventory that needs it passes. A block with no current reason, one tied to a resolved incident or ended campaign, or one applied network-wide when only a section needed it is a fail recorded with the block and the scope at issue.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active buyer, advertiser, brand, and category block with its reason and scope above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some of {{site_name}}'s buyer, advertiser, or brand block lists are broader than the brand-safety need that created them. Protective blocks are necessary, but each one also removes a potential bidder, and a block that outlived its reason or was scoped wider than it needed to be thins the competition Authorized Buyers brings and lowers the clearing price on the affected inventory, with no error to signal the loss. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each active block for a current, documented reason and narrowing any that apply network-wide when only a section needed the protection. After the change, watch the clearing price on the affected inventory for any lift as the freed demand competes again.
